elements.sent-2411-001: We study the space-time scaling limits of solitons in the box-ball system with random initial distribution.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we show that any recentered tagged soliton converges to a Brownian motion in the diffusive space-time scale, and also prove the large deviation principle for the tagged soliton under certain shift-ergodic invariant distributions, {including Bernoulli product measures and two-sided Markov distributions .}
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, in the diffusive space-time scaling, we show that two tagged solitons converge to the same Brownian motion even if they are macroscopically far apart.
elements.sent-2411-004: An integrable many-body system is a deterministic dynamical system consisting of an infinite type of quasi-local conserved quantities that behave like particles interacting with each other.
elements.sent-2411-005: These quasi-local conserved quantities are called quasi-particles.
elements.sent-2411-006: Solitary waves (solitons) in solitonic systems are examples of quasi-particles.
elements.sent-2411-007: Recently, integrable many-body systems have attracted much attention from the viewpoint of non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, and in particular, generalized hydrodynamics, which describes the macroscopic behavior of quasi-particles, see the reviews \cite{D,Sp and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-008: In the Euler space-time scale, it is expected that the hydrodynamics is described by the following generalized hydrodynamic equation (GHD equation) for $\mathbf{y (u,t) = \left( y_{a}\left(u,t\right)\right)_{a}$ of the universal form, regardless of models : \begin{equation}\partial_t y_{a}\left(u,t\right) + \partial_u \left( v^{eff}_{a}\left(y\left(u,t\right)\right)y_{a}\left(u,t\right) \right) = 0,\end{equation} where $y_{a}\left(u,t\right)$ is the macroscopic density of quasi-particles of type $a$ at macroscopic coordinate $(u,t), u \in \R, t \ge 0$, and $v^{\mathrm{eff _{a}$ is called the effective velocity of quasi-particles of type $a$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The specific form of the effective velocity depends on the scattering rule between quasi-particles, and this is where the differences among models arise.
elements.sent-2411-010: Although such studies have been rapidly developed in the physics literature, and the GHD theory is expected to be applicable for a wide class of integrable systems including classical and quantum gases, chains and field theory models, very few rigorous results are obtained till now, which are for the hard rods dynamics and its generalization \cite{BDS,FFGS , and the box-ball system (BBS) \cite{CS .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: While studies at the Euler scale have been progressing, there was no clear physical prediction on the behavior in a longer time scale.
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, it is important to obtain mathematically rigorous results on specific models in order to derive the universality for integrable many-body systems in the diffusive scale.
elements.sent-2411-013: Recently, by \cite{FO , the fluctuations for hard-rods in diffusive scale has been proved rigorously.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The difference from diffusive fluctuations in chaotic systems is the strong correlations between quasi-particles of the same type, i.e., { quasi-particles of the same type starting at macroscopic distance converge to the same Brownian motion.}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, the scattering rule in the hard-rods does not depend on the velocity of quasi-particles.
elements.sent-2411-016: This is a different feature from general integrable models, and no results have been known for the case where the scattering rule depends on velocities of the quasi-particles.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we consider the BBS, which is a solitonic system with a scattering rule depending on velocities of quasi-particles (solitons).
elements.sent-2411-018: We rigorously show that any tagged soliton converges to a Brownian motion in diffusive space-time scale, and also prove the large deviation principle for the tagged soliton.
elements.sent-2411-019: This is the first mathematical result { for the central limit theorem and the large deviation principle for quasi-particles in integrable systems with scattering rules depending on velocities of quasi-particles, unlike the hard-rods.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, {we rigorously prove that solitons of the same type converge to the same Brownian motion, i.e., strong correlations between quasi-particles as observed in the hard-rods.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In order to roughly describe the results, we first introduce the BBS below.
elements.sent-2411-022: The BBS is a one-dimensional cellular automaton introduced by \cite{TS , whose integrable structure has been extensively studied in the past, see the review \cite{IKT for details.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The BBS exhibits solitonic behavior and is understood as a discrete counterpart of the KdV equation, {which is a central example of an integrable system having solitary wave solutions .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The configuration space is $\left\{ 0, 1 \right\}^{\Z}$, where for $\eta \in \{0,1\}^{\Z}$ and $x \in \Z$, $\eta(x) = 1$ means that there is a ball at $x$, and $\eta(x) = 0$ means that $x$ is empty.
elements.sent-2411-025: When the total number of $1$s in $\eta \in \left\{ 0, 1 \right\}^{\Z}$ is finite, the one-step time evolution $\eta \mapsto T\eta \in \left\{ 0, 1 \right\}^{\Z}$ is described by the following rules :.
elements.sent-2411-026: An empty carrier enters the system from the left end (i.e. $-\infty$) and moves to the right {end (i.e. $\infty$) ;.}
elements.sent-2411-027: If there is a ball at site $x$, then the carrier picks up the ball;.
elements.sent-2411-028: If the site $x$ is empty and the carrier is not empty, then the carrier puts down a ball;.
elements.sent-2411-030: Clearly, the total number of $1$s are conserved.
elements.sent-2411-031: If we denote by $W(x)$ the number of balls on the carrier at $x$, then $W(\cdot)$ satisfies $W(x) = 0$ for any $|x| > L$ with sufficiently large $L > 0$, and \begin{equation}\label{def:carrier} W(x) - W(x-1) = \begin{dcases} 1  if  \eta(x) = 1, -1  if  \eta(x) = 0  and  W(x - 1) > 0, 0 otherwise. \end{dcases}\end{equation} In addition, $T\eta$ can be represented by using $W(\cdot)$ as \begin{equation}\label{def:T_carrier} T\eta(x) = \eta(x) - W(x) + W(x-1).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-181: For any $\gamma \in \Gamma_{k , \gamma' \in \Gamma_{\ell}$ with $k > \ell$, we say that $\gamma$ overtakes $\gamma'$ ( or $\gamma'$ is overtaken by $\gamma$) at time $n$ if $X\left(\gamma\left(n - 1\right)\right) < X\left(\gamma'\left(n - 1\right)\right)$ and $X\left(\gamma\left(n\right)\right) > X\left(\gamma'\left(n\right)\right)$.}

elements.sent-2411-183: It is shown by \cite[Proposition 6.4]{FNRW that for any $\gamma\in \Gamma_{k}$, the increment $X\left(\gamma(n)\right) - X\left(\gamma(n - 1)\right)$ can be represented as \begin{equation}X\left(\gamma(n)\right) - X\left(\gamma(n - 1)\right) = \begin{dcases} k + 2 \sum_{\ell = 1}^{k - 1} \ell N_{\ell}\left(\gamma,n\right) \ \ if $\gamma(n - 1)$ is free, 0 \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\label{eq:inc_sol}\end{equation} In particular, $X\left(\gamma(n)\right) - X\left(\gamma(n - 1)\right) > 0$ if and only if $\gamma(n - 1)$ is free.}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Seat number configuration for the box-ball system
elements.sent-2411-198: To derive the limiting behaviors of solitons, it is useful to consider seat number configuration space in which the dynamics of the BBS is linearized.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this section, we briefly recall the linearization method introduced by \cite{MSSS and seat by \cite{S .}}
elements.sent-2411-200: The main idea of this method is to assign a different parameter to each $0,1$ in $\eta \in \Omega$ based on the fact that $\eta$ contains many kinds of solitons.
elements.sent-2411-201: Then, we introduce a class of invariant measures for the BBS that are defined via the seat number configuration space.
elements.sent-2411-202: Throughout this subsection, we fix an $\eta \in \Omega$ arbitrarily.
elements.sent-2411-203: First, we introduce the notion of {\it carrier with seat numbers .}
elements.sent-2411-204: We consider a situation in which the {\it seats of the carrier $W$ are indexed by $k \in \N$, i.e, $W$ is decomposed as \begin{equation}W\left(\eta,x\right) := \sum_{k \in \N} W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right), \quad W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) \in \{0,1\},\end{equation} where $\mathcal{W _{k}\left(\eta,x\right) = 1$ means that the No.$k$ seat is occupied {when the carrier is at the site $x \in \mathbb{Z}$}.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Then, the refined update rule of such a carrier is given as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-206: If there is a ball at site $x$, then the carrier picks up the ball and puts it at the empty seat with the smallest seat number;.
elements.sent-2411-207: If the site $x$ is empty, namely $\eta(x)=0$, and if there is at least one occupied seat, then the carrier puts down the ball at the occupied seat with the smallest seat number;.
elements.sent-2411-208: Otherwise, the carrier just passes through.
elements.sent-2411-209: In other words, $\mathcal{W _{k , k \in \N$ are defined as $\mathcal{W _{k}\left(\eta,s_{\infty}\left(\eta,i\right)\right) := 0$ for any $i \in \Z$ and \begin{equation}W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) - W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \  if  \ \sum_{\ell = 1}^{k-1}W_{\ell}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 1, W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 0  and  \eta(x) = 1, -1 \ \  if  \ \sum_{\ell = 1}^{k-1}W_{\ell}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 0, W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 1  and  \eta(x) = 0, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases}\end{equation} and we call $\mathcal{W _{k , k \in \N$ the carrier with seat numbers.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation}W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) - W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \  if  \ \sum_{\ell = 1}^{k-1}W_{\ell}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 1, W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 0  and  \eta(x) = 1, -1 \ \  if  \ \sum_{\ell = 1}^{k-1}W_{\ell}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 0, W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 1  and  \eta(x) = 0, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-210: By using the carrier with seat numbers, we define the {\it seat number configuration $\eta^{\sigma _{k \in \Omega$, $\sigma \in \left\{ \uparrow, \downarrow \right\}$, $k \in \N$ and $r \in \{0,1 \}^{\Z}$ as \begin{equation}\eta^{\uparrow}_{k}\left(x\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) - W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 1, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases} \eta^{\downarrow}_{k}\left(x\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) - W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = -1, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}r\left(\eta,x\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ x = s_{\infty}\left(\eta,i\right)  for some  i \in \Z, 0 \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\end{equation} We note that by the seat number configuration, all $1, 0$ in $\eta$ are distinguished by the parameter $(k,\sigma)$ in the following sense : for any $x \in \Z$, \begin{equation}r(x) + \sum_{k \in \N} (\eta^{\uparrow}_{k}(x) + \eta^{\downarrow}_{k}(x)) = 1.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-035: \begin{equation}\eta^{\uparrow}_{k}\left(x\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) - W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = 1, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases} \eta^{\downarrow}_{k}\left(x\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ W_{k}\left(\eta,x\right) - W_{k}\left(\eta,x-1\right) = -1, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}r\left(\eta,x\right) := \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ x = s_{\infty}\left(\eta,i\right)  for some  i \in \Z, 0 \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-211: In the following, if a site $x \in \Z$ satisfies $\eta^{\sigma _{k}\left(x\right) = 1$ for some $k \in \N$ and $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$, then we call $x$ a $\left(k,\sigma\right)$-seat.}
elements.sent-2411-212: We note that the seat number configuration can be described in terms of solitons as follows, see \cite{MSSS, S for details. \begin{equation}\eta^{\uparrow}_{k}\left(x\right) = \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ x = H_{k}\left(\gamma\right)  for some  \gamma \in \bigcup_{\ell \ge k} \Gamma_{\ell}, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases} \eta^{\downarrow}_{k}\left(x\right) = \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ x = T_{k}\left(\gamma\right)  for some  \gamma \in \bigcup_{\ell \ge k} \Gamma_{\ell}, 0 \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\end{equation} In other words, a $k$-soliton consists of exactly one $\left(\ell,\sigma\right)$-seat for each $1 \le \ell \le k$ and $\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}$.}
elements.formula-2411-038: \begin{equation}\eta^{\uparrow}_{k}\left(x\right) = \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ x = H_{k}\left(\gamma\right)  for some  \gamma \in \bigcup_{\ell \ge k} \Gamma_{\ell}, 0 \ \ otherwise, \end{dcases} \eta^{\downarrow}_{k}\left(x\right) = \begin{dcases} 1 \ \ x = T_{k}\left(\gamma\right)  for some  \gamma \in \bigcup_{\ell \ge k} \Gamma_{\ell}, 0 \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-213: Then, by using the above configurations, for each $k \in \N$, we define a non-decreasing function $\xi_{k}\left(\ \cdot \ \right) : \Z \to \Z $ and its inverse {(for a certain sense) $s_{k}\left(\ \cdot \ \right) : \Z \to \Z $ as \begin{equation}\xi_{k}\left( \eta, x \right) - \xi_{k}\left( \eta, x - 1 \right) := r\left(\eta,x\right) + \sum_{\ell \in \N} \sum_{\sigma \in \{\uparrow, \downarrow\}} \eta^{\sigma}_{k + \ell}\left( x \right), \xi_{k}\left(\eta, s_{\infty}\left(\eta,0\right) \right) := 0,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{def:s_k} s_{k}\left(\eta, x \right) := \min \left\{ y \in \Z \ ; \ \xi_{k}\left(\eta,y \right) = x \right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-214: The intuitive meaning of $\xi_{k}$ is that it is a function that counts the number of $1$s and $0$s from the reference point $s_{\infty (0)$, ignoring solitons of {size $k$ or less, and ignoring up to the k-th $1$s and $0$s constituting solitons, see Remark \ref{rem:seat_soliton .}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: This counting method allows us to measure the effective distance between solitons, see Remark \ref{rem:eff_dis .}
elements.sent-2411-216: Finally, for any $k \in \Z$, we define $\zeta_{k : \Z \to \Z_{\ge 0}$ as \begin{equation}\zeta_{k}\left(\eta,i\right) := \sum_{y = s_{k}\left(\eta,i\right) + 1}^{s_{k}\left(\eta,i + 1\right)} \left( \eta^{\uparrow}_{k}\left(y\right) - \eta^{\uparrow}_{k+1}\left(y\right) \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-217: We emphasize three important properties about $\zeta_{k}$ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-218: The first is that the function $\zeta_{k}$ and $k$-soltions are related via the following formula, \begin{equation}\zeta_{k}\left(\eta,i\right) = \left| \left\{ \gamma \in \Gamma_{k}\left(\eta\right) \ ; \ \gamma \subset \left[ s_{k}\left(\eta,i\right), s_{k}\left(\eta,i+1\right) \right] \right\} \right|,\end{equation} i.e., $\zeta_{k}$ represents the total number of $k$-solitons satisfying $\xi_{k (\eta,X(\gamma)) = i$.}
elements.sent-2411-219: In particular, our $\zeta$ coincides with the slot decomposition introduced in \cite{FNRW , see \cite[Section 2.1, Proposition 2.3]{MSSS and \cite[Section 4.1]{S for details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-220: From the same reasons as in the discussion just before Lemma \ref{lem:sp_shift , we can see that for any $k \in \N$, $i \in \Z$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:zeta_shift} \zeta_{k}\left( \tau_{s_{\infty}(0)} \eta, i\right) = \zeta_{k}\left( \eta, i\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-221: The second is the bijectivity {between $\zeta$ and $\eta$ satisfying $s_{\infty (0)=0$, namely the configuration such that the origin is a record .}}
elements.sent-2411-222: We define {the space of such configurations $\Omega_{0 \subset \Omega$, {and also introduce $\bar{\Omega \subset \Z_{\ge 0 ^{\mathbb{N \times \Z}$ as \begin{equation}\Omega_{0} := \Omega \cap \left\{ s_{\infty}\left(0\right) = 0 \right\} \bar{\Omega} := \left\{ \zeta \in \Z_{\ge 0}^{N \times \Z} \ ; \ \sum_{k \in \N} \zeta_{k}\left(i\right) < \infty \ for any  i \right\}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: It is known that $\zeta : \Omega_{0 \to \bar{\Omega}$ is a bijection, {see \cite[Section 3]{FNRW for details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: We note that we can not reconstruct the original $\eta$ from $(\zeta_{k (\eta, \ \cdot \ ))_{k \in \N}$ in general, because there is an arbitrariness in the choice of the position of $s_{\infty (0)$ from \eqref{eq:zeta_shift , see also Figure \ref{fig:diag_eta_zeta for a summary of these properties, where for any $\eta \in \Omega$, we write \begin{equation}\label{def:palm_eta} \tilde{\eta} := \tau_{s_{\infty}(0)} \eta \in \Omega_{0}.\end{equation}.}}}}

The arrows $\leftrightarrow$ represent certain bijections, and the arrow $\rightarrow$ represents the first coordinate projection.} \label{fig:diag_eta_zeta}
elements.sent-2411-225: The third is that the dynamics of the BBS can be linearized via $\zeta_{k}$ with a certain offset \cite{FNRW, S .}
elements.sent-2411-226: Here, we cite the result by \cite{S for later use.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Then, for any $k \in \N$ and $i \in \Z$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:linear_seat} \zeta_{k}\left(T\eta, i + k + o_{k}\left(\eta\right) \right) = \zeta_{k}\left(\eta, i \right),\end{equation} where the offset $o_{k}\left(\eta\right)$ is given by \begin{equation}o_{k}\left(\eta\right) := s_{\infty}\left(\eta,0\right) - s_{\infty}\left( T\eta,0\right) + 2\sum_{y = s_{\infty}\left(0\right)+ 1}^{0} \sum_{\ell = 1}^{k} \eta^{\downarrow}_{\ell}\left(y\right) - 2\sum_{y = Ts_{\infty}\left(0\right)+1}^{0}\sum_{\ell = 1}^{k} T\eta^{\uparrow}_{\ell}\left(y\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-228: We note that if $s_{\infty}\left(0\right) = 0$ and any soliton do not cross the origin $x = 0$ {in the evolution from $\eta$ to $T\eta$}, then $o_{k = 0$ for any $k \in \N$.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Hence, if $\eta \left(x\right) = 0$ for any $x \le 0$, then \begin{equation}\zeta_{k}\left(T^n\eta, i + nk\right) = \zeta_{k}\left(\eta,i\right),\end{equation} for any $k \in \N$, $n \in \N$ and $i \in \Z$.
elements.sent-2411-230: The above equation reproduces the linearization result for the BBS on $\left\{0,1 \right\}^{\N}$ shown in \cite{MSSS .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Later in this paper we will use the following lemma, which can be considered as a version of \eqref{eq:linear_seat .}
elements.sent-2411-232: The proof will be given in Section \ref{app:lem_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-233: For any $k \ge \ell$, $i \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $n \in \N$, we have \begin{equation}\xi_{\ell}\left( T^{n}\eta, X^{(i)}_{k}\left(n\right) \right) - \xi_{\ell}\left( T^{n-1}\eta, X^{(i)}_{k}\left(n-1\right) \right) = \begin{dcases} k + o_{\ell}\left(T^{n-1}\eta\right) + 2 \sum_{h = \ell + 1}^{k - 1}(h - \ell) N^{(i)}_{k,h}\left(\eta, n\right) \ \ if  \gamma^{(i)}_{k}(n-1)  is free, \ell + o_{\ell}\left(T^{n-1}\eta\right) \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\label{eq:dif_xi}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-049: \begin{equation}\xi_{\ell}\left( T^{n}\eta, X^{(i)}_{k}\left(n\right) \right) - \xi_{\ell}\left( T^{n-1}\eta, X^{(i)}_{k}\left(n-1\right) \right) = \begin{dcases} k + o_{\ell}\left(T^{n-1}\eta\right) + 2 \sum_{h = \ell + 1}^{k - 1}(h - \ell) N^{(i)}_{k,h}\left(\eta, n\right) \ \ if  \gamma^{(i)}_{k}(n-1)  is free, \ell + o_{\ell}\left(T^{n-1}\eta\right) \ \ otherwise. \end{dcases}\label{eq:dif_xi}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-234: For each $k \in \N$, we define the $k$-th effective distance between $\gamma, \gamma' \in \Gamma_{k}$ as \begin{equation}d_{eff,k}\left(\eta,\gamma, \gamma'\right) := \left| \xi_{k}\left(\eta,X\left(\gamma\right)\right) - \xi_{k}\left(\eta,X\left(\gamma'\right)\right) \right|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-235: Then, we see that \begin{equation}d_{eff,k}\left(\eta,\gamma, \gamma'\right) = 0  if and only if  \gamma' \in Con\left(\gamma\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-236: In addition, from Lemma \ref{lem:dif_xi , we see that the effective distance is conserved in time, i.e., for any $\gamma, \gamma' \in \Gamma_{k}$ and $n \in \Z_{\ge 0}$, we have \begin{equation}d_{eff,k}\left(T^n\eta,\gamma\left(n\right), \gamma'\left(n\right) \right) = \left| \xi_{k}\left(T^n\eta , X\left(\gamma\left(n\right)\right)\right) - \xi_{k}\left(T^n\eta, X\left(\gamma'\left(n\right)\right)\right) \right| = d_{eff,k}\left(\eta,\gamma, \gamma'\right),\end{equation} and thus we get \begin{equation}\left|T^n Con\left( \gamma\left(n\right) \right)\right| = \left|Con\left( \gamma \right)\right|.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-237: In this section we recall a class of invariant measures for the BBS, introduced by \cite{FG .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Then, we consider scaling limits for solitons starting from such invariant measures.
elements.sent-2411-239: We recall a class of translation-invariant stationary measures on $\left\{0,1 \right\}^{\Z}$ introduced by \cite{FG .}
elements.sent-2411-240: We define a set of infinite number of parameters as follows : \begin{equation}Q := \left\{ q = \left(q_k\right)_{k \in \N} \in [0,1)^{\N} \ ; \ \sum_{k \in \N} k q_{k} < \infty \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-241: From \cite[Theorem 4.4, 4.5]{FG , for given $\mathbf{q \in \mathcal{Q}$, there exists a translation-invariant stationary measure $\nu_{\mathbf{q }$ such that $\left(\zeta_{k}\left(i\right)\right)_{k \in \N, i \in \Z}$ are i.i.d.}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: In the following, we call $\nu_\mathbf{q}$ the $q$-statistics.
elements.sent-2411-243: For later use, we recall {some properties of $\nu_{\mathbf{q }$ in the following remark.}}
elements.sent-2411-244: Recall that the $i$-th excursion $\mathbf{e ^{(i) (\eta)$ in $\eta$ is defined in \eqref{def:excursion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: These excursions are elements of the set $\mathcal{E}$ given by \begin{equation}E := \cup_{m \in \Z_{\ge 0}} E(m), E(m) := \left\{ e \in \{ 0, 1 \}^{2m + 1} \ ; \ \sup_{1 \le y \le 2m+1}\sum_{x = 1}^{y} \left( 2e(x) - 1 \right) \le -1, \ \sum_{x = 1}^{2m+1} e(x) = m \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-055: \begin{equation}E := \cup_{m \in \Z_{\ge 0}} E(m), E(m) := \left\{ e \in \{ 0, 1 \}^{2m + 1} \ ; \ \sup_{1 \le y \le 2m+1}\sum_{x = 1}^{y} \left( 2e(x) - 1 \right) \le -1, \ \sum_{x = 1}^{2m+1} e(x) = m \right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-246: For each $i \in \Z$, $\mathbf{e ^{(i)}\left(\eta\right)$ can be considered as an $\mathcal{E}$-valued random variable under $\nu_{\mathbf{q }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: Then, from the explicit construction of $\nu_{\mathbf{q }$ in \cite[Section 4]{FG , $(\mathbf{e ^{(i) )_{i \in \Z}$ are an i.i.d.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Let $\text{Ber}\left(\rho\right)$ be the Bernoulli product measure on $\left\{0,1 \right\}^{\Z}$ with intensity $0 < \rho < 1/2$.
